topic: A person learns their childhood best friend doesn't remember them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The coffee shop buzzed with the usual midday chaos, but my focus was entirely on Liam. He was laughing, a bright, easy sound that always used to be reserved just for us. He was talking animatedly about a recent trip, completely oblivious to the slight tension radiating off me. I watched him, a tight knot forming in my chest. I had spent the last hour recounting a ridiculously specific memory from our seventh-grade science fair, a moment that, to me, was the bedrock of our friendship. When I finally spoke, my voice came out sharper than intended, a little too loud over the clatter of mugs. He turned to me, his smile faltering just a fraction, a flicker of confusion crossing his eyes before he quickly moved on to another topic. I just stared into my lukewarm latte, the ceramic suddenly feeling hot against my palm. *Of course,* I thought, a slow, sinking realization spreading through me. *He doesn't recall anything.*

[story 2]

I leaned against the brick wall, crossing my arms so tightly my knuckles turned white. Sarah was recounting a story about her weekend, and I tried to keep my expression neutral, a practiced mask of polite interest. But every time she referenced 'that summer,' a cold wave washed over me. I watched Liam across the room, animatedly recounting a joke, his head tilted back in amusement. I took a deep, slow breath, trying to steady the frantic drumming beneath my ribs. When Sarah mentioned planning a reunion, a strange, hollow feeling settled in my stomach. I forced a smile that felt brittle on my face. "Yeah, that sounds fun," I managed, the words tasting like ash. Inside, a sharp, impatient energy coiled up. Why was this so difficult? Why did this simple fact feel like a physical blow? I shifted my weight, the movement jerky, wanting nothing more than to walk out of the building and escape the suffocating quiet of my own disbelief.

[story 3]

It was a quiet evening at our shared apartment, the kind of comfortable silence that usually settled over us like a warm blanket. Tonight, it felt suffocating. Liam was fiddling with his phone, oblivious to the strained atmosphere. I watched the way his brow furrowed in concentration, the sheer normalcy of his engagement grating on my nerves. I finally decided to address it, needing to puncture the thick silence. "Remember that time we tried to build that ridiculous fort in the backyard?" I asked, my tone carefully modulated, trying to keep the edge out of my voice. He looked up, blinked slowly, and then just shrugged, a vague, unengaged gesture. His response was so utterly blank, so devoid of shared history, it felt like a deliberate erasure. I let out a short, sharp exhale, the air leaving my lungs in a frustrated puff. I just shook my head, a small, tight motion, and turned away, needing distance from the sheer absurdity of the situation.
